name: Acute Diarrhoea (Adult)
overview: Acute onset of loose or watery stools, three or more times per day, lasting less than 14 days. Most cases in adults are self-limiting viral or bacterial gastroenteritis requiring supportive care only.
howToDiagnose:
Clinical: sudden onset loose/watery stools, crampy abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, low-grade fever
Red flag features that suggest bacterial: bloody stool, high fever >38.5°C, severe abdominal pain, immunocompromised host, recent antibiotic use, recent travel
Investigations (if red flags present): stool culture and sensitivity, CBC, CRP, renal function (if signs of dehydration)
Dehydration assessment: skin turgor, mucous membranes, urine output, BP, HR
examPearls:
Assess hydration status at every visit — it guides management more than aetiology
Tympanitic abdomen with hyperactive bowel sounds is typical
Rebound tenderness or rigidity suggests surgical cause — refer immediately
Orthostatc hypotension indicates significant dehydration
redFlags:
Bloody or mucoid stool — suggests invasive bacterial cause
High fever above 38.5°C with systemic toxicity
Signs of severe dehydration: sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, tachycardia, hypotension
No urine output for 8+ hours
Abdominal rigidity or rebound tenderness
Symptoms persisting beyond 7 days without improvement
Age over 70 with multiple comorbidities
Immunocompromised patient
referral:
Severe dehydration requiring IV fluids — admit
Bloody diarrhoea with systemic toxicity — admit
Suspected surgical cause — urgent surgical referral
Diarrhoea lasting more than 14 days — investigate for chronic cause
Immunocompromised patient with any diarrhoea — lower threshold for admission
duration: Most viral cases resolve in 3–5 days. Bacterial cases 5–7 days with antibiotics if indicated.
source: WHO Diarrhoea Treatment Guidelines 2023, IDSA Clinical Infectious Diseases Guidelines

name: Acute Constipation
overview: Infrequent bowel movements (fewer than three per week), difficulty passing stool, or a sensation of incomplete evacuation. Acute constipation in adults is most commonly functional, related to diet, fluid intake, inactivity, or medications.
howToDiagnose:
Clinical: Rome IV criteria — two or more of: straining, lumpy/hard stool, incomplete evacuation, anorectal obstruction, manual manoeuvres, fewer than 3 bowel movements per week — for at least 25% of defecations
History: duration, stool consistency (Bristol scale), diet, fluid intake, medications (opioids, iron, calcium, antidepressants, antacids)
Exam: abdominal palpation for faecal loading, digital rectal examination for rectal impaction
Red flag history: rectal bleeding, unexplained weight loss, change in bowel habit over 50 years, family history of colorectal cancer, iron deficiency anaemia
examPearls:
Always ask about medications — opioids, iron supplements, and calcium channel blockers are common culprits
Palpable colon on abdominal exam suggests significant faecal loading
Digital rectal exam is essential if impaction suspected or if patient is elderly
Empty rectum on DRE with loaded colon suggests slow transit — escalate if no response to laxatives
redFlags:
Rectal bleeding — especially fresh blood or melaena
Unexplained significant weight loss
New change in bowel habit after age 50
Severe abdominal pain or distension — suspect obstruction
Absent bowel sounds — surgical emergency
Faecal impaction unresponsive to oral laxatives
Constipation in setting of thyroid disease or diabetes that is uncontrolled
referral:
Suspected colorectal malignancy — urgent two-week-wait referral
Complete bowel obstruction — emergency surgical referral
Faecal impaction requiring manual evacuation — refer to acute assessment
Failing to respond to adequate laxative trial after 4–6 weeks — specialist gastroenterology referral
duration: Acute episode typically resolves within 3–7 days with appropriate laxative use. Lifestyle measures are ongoing.
source: British Society of Gastroenterology Constipation Guidelines 2022, NICE CG61

name: Acute Cough — URTI with Cough
nameAr: السعال الحاد — التهاب الجهاز التنفسي العلوي مع سعال
overview: Acute cough is the most common presenting complaint in primary care. In the context of URTI it is almost always viral, self-limiting, and does not require antibiotics. Treatment is symptomatic to reduce distress and improve sleep.
howToDiagnose:
Clinical: sudden onset cough, coryzal symptoms (runny or blocked nose), sore throat, low-grade fever, myalgia — all suggesting viral aetiology
Duration: acute if less than 3 weeks
Character: dry or productive (viral cough can be both)
Investigations: not routinely required for acute URTI cough
CRP: consider if uncertain about bacterial vs viral — CRP <20 strongly viral, CRP >100 suggests bacterial
Chest X-ray: only if signs of lower respiratory tract involvement (reduced air entry, crackles, dullness to percussion, oxygen saturation <94%)
Fever pattern and sputum colour are poor predictors of bacterial infection
examPearls:
Auscultate chest at every visit — clear chest with no added sounds supports viral URTI diagnosis and avoids unnecessary antibiotics
Post-nasal drip can cause persistent dry cough — look for cobblestone posterior pharynx
Expiratory wheeze with cough suggests reactive airways — consider bronchodilator trial
Stridor suggests upper airway involvement — refer urgently
redFlags:
Oxygen saturation below 94% at rest
Respiratory rate above 24 per minute in adults
High fever above 39°C persisting beyond 3 days
Haemoptysis — any amount
Signs of consolidation on chest examination
Severe dyspnoea at rest or on minimal exertion
Cough persisting beyond 3 weeks without improvement
Night sweats and weight loss with persistent cough — rule out tuberculosis
Immunocompromised patient
referral:
Suspected pneumonia — admit or urgent chest X-ray depending on severity
Haemoptysis — urgent respiratory referral
Persistent cough beyond 8 weeks — investigate for underlying cause
Suspected tuberculosis — refer to respiratory/infectious disease immediately
Croup with stridor — paediatric emergency
duration: Viral URTI cough typically resolves in 10–21 days. Antitussives provide symptomatic relief only.
source: NICE NG120 Cough 2019, NICE CKS Acute Cough 2023, SIGN Guidelines

name: Mild to Moderate Acute Pain
overview: Acute pain scoring 1–6 on the numerical rating scale, arising from musculoskeletal, headache, dental, or post-procedural causes. Management follows the WHO analgesic ladder with paracetamol and NSAIDs as first-line agents.
howToDiagnose:
Assess: location, onset, character, radiation, severity (NRS 1–10), aggravating and relieving factors, duration
NRS 1–3: mild pain — paracetamol alone usually sufficient
NRS 4–6: moderate pain — NSAID alone or NSAID plus paracetamol
NRS 7–10: severe pain — beyond scope of this template
Consider cause: musculoskeletal, headache, post-procedural, dental — treat the underlying cause alongside analgesia
Red flags for specific pain types must be excluded before prescribing analgesics — do not mask pain from a surgical or serious cause
examPearls:
Always examine the site of pain — analgesics should not be prescribed without at least considering the source
Tenderness over a joint with swelling may suggest gout or septic arthritis — both need specific management
Headache with neck stiffness, photophobia, or fever — do not give analgesia and discharge — urgent meningitis workup
Abdominal pain — ensure peritonism excluded before prescribing NSAIDs
NSAIDs: check renal function, history of peptic ulcer, and cardiovascular risk before prescribing
redFlags:
Pain score 7 or above — requires stronger analgesia beyond this template
Headache with: neck stiffness, photophobia, fever, thunderclap onset, new neurological signs
Chest pain — must exclude cardiac cause before treating as musculoskeletal
Abdominal pain with guarding or rebound tenderness — surgical cause
Back pain with: saddle anaesthesia, urinary or bowel incontinence, bilateral leg weakness — cauda equina emergency
Pain not responding to adequate dose of first-line analgesia after 48 hours
referral:
NRS 7+ not controlled with oral analgesia — consider parenteral or hospital-based pain management
Pain from suspected malignancy — oncology/palliative care
Chronic pain developing from acute cause — pain specialist referral
Any red flag feature — refer to appropriate specialty urgently
duration: Analgesics prescribed for the minimum effective duration. Acute musculoskeletal pain: 5–7 days. Post-procedural: 2–3 days. Re-evaluate if not improving.
source: WHO Analgesic Ladder, BNF 2024, NICE CKS Analgesia 2023

name: Uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infection (Adult Female)
nameAr: التهاب المسالك البولية غير المعقد (المرأة البالغة)
overview: Acute bacterial infection of the lower urinary tract (bladder and urethra) in a non-pregnant, immunocompetent adult female with no structural or functional urinary tract abnormality. Escherichia coli accounts for 80–85% of cases.
howToDiagnose:
Clinical diagnosis is sufficient in a non-pregnant female with classic triad: dysuria, frequency, urgency — with or without suprapubic pain
Dipstick urinalysis: positive leucocyte esterase and nitrites supports diagnosis
MSU culture: not routinely required for uncomplicated first presentation — reserve for: treatment failure, recurrent UTI (2+ in 6 months), atypical symptoms, pregnant women, suspected upper UTI
Complicated UTI features that change management: fever above 38°C, loin pain, rigors, nausea/vomiting, pregnant, male patient, catheterised, structural abnormality, immunosuppressed, diabetes, recent hospital admission
examPearls:
Suprapubic tenderness on palpation confirms lower UTI localisation
Costovertebral angle tenderness (renal angle) indicates upper UTI / pyelonephritis — escalate management immediately
Absence of fever and systemic features is reassuring for lower UTI
Vaginal discharge or dyspareunia suggests STI or vaginitis — consider swabs before treating as UTI
Post-coital UTI in younger women is extremely common — mention prophylaxis option at follow-up
redFlags:
Fever above 38°C with loin pain — pyelonephritis, consider admission
Rigors or systemic sepsis features — admit urgently
Haematuria — macro or micro — after UTI resolved, investigate further
Recurrent UTI more than twice in 6 months — investigate and consider prophylaxis
Symptoms not improving after 48 hours of antibiotics — consider culture, resistance, or upper UTI
Pregnancy — any UTI requires treatment and different antibiotic choice
Male patient — UTI is always complicated in men — refer or investigate
referral:
Suspected pyelonephritis with systemic features — consider hospital admission
UTI in pregnant women — treat and refer to antenatal care
Recurrent UTI — urology referral for cystoscopy and imaging
Haematuria persisting after UTI resolution — haematuria clinic
Male UTI — urology referral to exclude prostate, structural, or renal cause
duration: Uncomplicated lower UTI: 3 days (trimethoprim) or 7 days (nitrofurantoin). Single-dose regimens available for some antibiotics. Complicated UTI: 7–14 days.
source: NICE NG109 UTI in Adults 2022, SIGN 88 Management of Suspected Bacterial UTI in Adults, EAU Guidelines on Urological Infections 2023
